messages.speaker: Dev
messages.content: you're mine too Cas. always. ❤️

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Hey, did you end up going to that breakout session on data pipelines?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Yeah! Actually it was pretty good. The speaker was from Datadog.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Oh nice, I skipped it for the leadership one. Kinda wish I hadn't.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Haha was it bad?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Not bad exactly, just very corporate-speak. Lots of 'synergy' and 'alignment.'

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh god, the alignment talk. I swear every offsite has one of those.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Right? Anyway, I think we're in the same group for the afternoon thing.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh yeah the team-building exercise? I saw the list.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yeah. Should be interesting at least.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Honestly I'm just glad to be out of the office. Vanessa's been on a tear lately.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Oh my god, tell me about it. She pulled me into a 'strategy session' Friday that was basically her rethinking the entire Q4 roadmap.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Wait, again? Didn't she just finalize the roadmap like two weeks ago?
messages.notes: Temporal: roadmap was finalized 2 weeks prior

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yep. Two weeks. The ink wasn't even dry.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: That's wild. I feel like she does this every quarter.
messages.notes: Casual observation about Vanessa's behavior pattern

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: She does. It's exhausting. What do you think about the way she handles sprint planning in general?
messages.notes: Pat's analytical question about management style

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Honestly? I think she means well but she changes direction so fast that nobody can actually execute.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: That's exactly it. The team can't build momentum when the target keeps moving.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Yeah. Anyway, at least the food here is good.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Ha, true. The coffee is actually decent for once.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Right? I've had like three cups already. I'm going to be wired for the afternoon session.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Same. So what do you usually do on weekends? I realized we've worked together for like 8 months and I barely know anything about you outside of standups.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Ha, that's so true. Honestly I've been training a lot lately. I'm doing the Portland Marathon in April.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Wait, really? That's awesome. Have you done a marathon before?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Nope, this would be my first. I've done a bunch of halfs though. The last one was the Seattle Half in September, did a 1:42.
messages.notes: First marathon, Seattle Half in Sept, time 1:42

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Is that good? I honestly know nothing about running.
messages.notes: Casual admission: Pat knows nothing about running

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Ha, yeah it's decent. I'm trying to go sub-3:30 for the full.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: What do you think about the training load? Like how much time does it actually take?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Right now I'm doing about 40 miles a week. Five days running, two rest days. It's a lot honestly.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Wow, that's serious commitment. I could barely run a mile without dying.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Haha it's not for everyone. What about you? Any hobbies outside of work?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I've been reading a lot about startups lately, actually. Like obsessively.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh interesting. Like what kind of stuff?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Mostly analytics and data tooling. I keep seeing gaps in the market that our team runs into every day.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Huh. Are you thinking about building something?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Maybe? I don't know. It's just an idea at this point. I read this article on Indie Hackers about a guy who built a $30K MRR analytics tool as a solo founder.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: That's cool. You definitely have the brain for it.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Thanks. I'll probably just keep thinking about it for another year and never do anything, knowing me.
messages.notes: Self-aware emotional disclosure about inaction tendency

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Ha, that's literally everyone with a side project idea. Join the club.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Fair point.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Hey I think they're calling us back for the afternoon thing.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Oh right. Which room are we in?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Conference B I think. Second floor.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Cool, I'll head over. Nice actually talking to you outside of standup for once.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Ha, same. We should do this more often.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Definitely.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh hey, random question — do you know if the offsite dinner tonight is mandatory?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I think it's 'strongly encouraged,' which at this company means yes.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Ugh. Fine. At least it's at that Italian place on 4th. Lucca's.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Oh nice, I've actually heard good things about that place.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Yeah their pasta is supposed to be incredible. OK see you in Conference B.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Are you in the office today?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Yeah I'm at my desk. What's up?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Did you see the email from Vanessa?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Which one? She sent like 4 this morning.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: The one about pivoting the dashboard project. She wants to completely rethink the metrics layer.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh that one. Yeah I read it twice trying to figure out what she actually wants.
messages.notes: Context-enabling: Riley's confusion about the email

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: The demo to the VP is in two weeks, Riley. TWO WEEKS.
messages.notes: VP demo in two weeks, deadline pressure

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: I know. It's... ambitious.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: It's insane is what it is. We scoped this thing out in August. The architecture is already built.
messages.notes: Project scoped in August, architecture complete

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Yeah I was there for the design review. We spent like a whole sprint just on the schema.
messages.notes: Temporal: design review was a full sprint

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Exactly. And now she wants to swap out the entire aggregation pipeline because she read some blog post about 'real-time analytics.'
messages.notes: Vanessa's reason: blog post about real-time analytics

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Lol wait, a blog post? Please tell me you're exaggerating.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I wish I was. She literally linked it in the email. It was a Medium article.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh no. The Medium article pipeline. Classic Vanessa.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: What do you think I should do? Push back or just try to make it work?
messages.notes: Pat asking for advice, analytical framing

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Honestly? Push back. But tactfully. Frame it as risk to the demo timeline.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yeah that's what I was thinking. I need to quantify the cost of the pivot.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: There you go. She responds to numbers. If you can show her it adds 3 weeks of work, she'll probably back down.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: True. She does respond to data better than opinions.
messages.notes: Casual insight: Vanessa responds to data

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Meanwhile I'm just over here trying to finish the code review backlog before standup tomorrow.
messages.notes: Temporal: code review before tomorrow's standup

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Ugh, the code review backlog. I have like 6 PRs waiting too.
messages.notes: Casual fact: Pat has 6 PRs in queue

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Sprint planning is going to be a blast this week.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Can't wait.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Anyway. You'll figure it out with Vanessa. You always do.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Thanks. Hey, actually — remember how I mentioned at the offsite that I've been reading about analytics tools?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Yeah, the Indie Hackers stuff?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: So I've actually been thinking about it more seriously. Like, what if I actually built something?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh wow, really? What would it be?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: An analytics SaaS tool. Specifically for mid-size teams that outgrow Google Analytics but can't afford Amplitude or Mixpanel.
messages.notes: Business idea: analytics SaaS for mid-size teams

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh that's actually a real gap. We dealt with that exact problem at my last company.
messages.notes: Validation: Riley experienced the same gap

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Right?? That's what got me thinking. Every mid-size company I've worked at has this exact problem.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Do you have a name for it yet?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Not yet. I'm still in the 'is this actually viable' phase. I've been doing customer interviews on the side.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Wait you're already doing customer interviews? That's not 'just an idea' anymore.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Ha, I guess not. I've talked to like 6 people so far. Mostly product managers and growth leads.
messages.notes: Interview count: 6, target personas: PMs and growth leads

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: And what are they saying?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Every single one said the same thing: they need something between GA and the enterprise tools. Something that does event analytics without a $50K annual contract.
messages.notes: Customer interview finding: need for mid-tier pricing

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Dude that's really promising. Have you thought about pricing?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Not in detail yet. But the sweet spot seems to be somewhere around $30-60 per month per seat.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: That sounds right. Low enough to expense without VP approval.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Exactly. The 'just put it on the company card' price point.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Ha! That's literally a business strategy. I love it.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Thanks for actually taking this seriously, by the way. I haven't told many people.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Of course. I think you should go for it honestly.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: We'll see. One existential work crisis at a time.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Lol fair. OK I really need to go look at those PRs. Talk later?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yeah for sure. And thanks for the Vanessa advice. I'll write up the risk analysis tonight.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Smart. Good luck with it. Let me know how it goes.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Will do. Thanks Riley.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Happy Monday. How'd the Vanessa thing go?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Actually really well! I sent her the risk analysis Friday and she backed off the full pivot.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh nice! So no last-minute architecture rework?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: She still wants some changes but nothing that blows up the timeline. We compromised on adding two new metric views instead of redoing the whole pipeline.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: See, I told you. Numbers work on her.
messages.notes: Context-enabling: callback to own earlier advice

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: You were right. I even put together a Gantt chart showing the impact and she was like 'oh I didn't realize it was that much work.'
messages.notes: Casual fact: used a Gantt chart

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Lmao of course she didn't. Managers never do.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Ha. True. So how was your weekend? Did you get your long run in?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: YES and it was a good one actually. Did 16 miles Saturday along the river trail.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Sixteen miles?? That's like... a lot.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Ha yeah. It's my longest run so far in this training cycle. The Portland Marathon is April 12th so I need to peak around late March.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: How do you even train for something like that? Is there like a plan you follow?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Yeah I'm doing the Pfitzinger 18/55 plan. It's an 18-week program with 5 runs per week, building up to 55 miles in the peak week.
messages.notes: Training plan: Pfitzinger 18/55, 18 weeks, 55 peak miles

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: That's incredibly structured. I respect the data-driven approach.
messages.notes: Casual fact: Pat appreciates data-driven methods

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Ha, you would appreciate the data side. I track everything in Strava — splits, heart rate, elevation.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Of course you do. What do you think about the mental side of it? Like how do you stay motivated on the bad days?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Honestly? Some days I don't. I just go anyway and hate every second of it. And then I feel amazing after.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: That's kind of inspiring actually.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Don't tell anyone I said something inspiring. I have a brand to maintain.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Ha, your secret is safe with me.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: So what about you? How's the analytics thing going? Have you done more interviews?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: OK so this is actually exciting. I've now talked to 12 people total and the pattern is really clear.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I feel like I've validated the core hypothesis. There's a real gap between free tools and enterprise solutions.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: 12 interviews is no joke. So what's the next step?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I want to build an MVP. Nothing fancy — just event tracking, a simple dashboard, and basic funnels.
messages.notes: MVP scope: event tracking, dashboard, funnels

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Are you going to build it yourself or find a cofounder?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Myself for now. I can handle the backend and I found this React template that'll save time on the frontend.
messages.notes: Solo build, React template for frontend

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: When do you think you can have something working?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: If I'm disciplined about it, maybe 6-8 weeks? I've been doing about 2 hours a night after work.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Two hours a night PLUS the day job? That's intense.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yeah it's a lot. But honestly it doesn't feel like work. It's the first thing in a long time that actually energizes me.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: That's how you know it's the right thing. When it doesn't feel like a slog.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: What do you think about the name 'Clearview Analytics'?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Hmm. It's fine but there's a facial recognition company called Clearview AI. Might cause confusion.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Oh good call. Back to the drawing board on that one.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: What about something shorter? Like Beacon or Tally or Signal?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Hmm, those are interesting. I'll think about it.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Hey totally different topic — did you get Jason's wedding invite?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yes! Jason and Elena, right? December 14th in Napa Valley.
messages.notes: Wedding: Jason & Elena, Dec 14, Napa Valley

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Yeah! Are you going?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Definitely. I've known Jason since college. How do you know them?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Elena and I worked together at my first job out of school. She was my desk neighbor for two years.
messages.notes: Riley's connection to Elena: first job, desk neighbors

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Oh that's funny. I didn't realize we had mutual friends outside of work.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Small world! I'm excited for the wedding honestly. Napa in December is gorgeous.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yeah I heard they booked this vineyard called Domaine Chandon. It's supposed to be beautiful.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh I've been there for a tasting. It's stunning.
messages.notes: Casual fact: Riley has visited the venue

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Nice. Have you thought about a gift yet?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Not yet. They're registered at Crate & Barrel and Williams-Sonoma I think.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: OK cool. I'll look at those. What do you think about going in on something together? Might be nice to get them something bigger.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Oh that's a great idea actually. Let me pull up the registry.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yeah let's figure it out closer to the date. No rush.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: For sure. We can look at the registry together sometime this week.
messages.notes: Temporal: planning to look this week

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Sounds good. OK I need to hop on my 2pm. Talk later?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yeah go. And good luck on your next long run.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Thanks! 🏃

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I'm going to lose it.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Uh oh. Vanessa?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Who else. She called an emergency meeting this morning to change the requirements for the demo AGAIN.
messages.notes: Vanessa: emergency meeting, changed demo requirements

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: You're kidding. The demo is next Thursday.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I am not kidding. She wants us to add a real-time notification system now. Real-time. In one week.
messages.notes: New requirement: real-time notifications in one week

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: That's... not feasible.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I know! I told her that. She said 'I believe in this team's ability to deliver under pressure.'
messages.notes: Vanessa's response: 'ability to deliver under pressure'

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Ah yes, the classic 'I believe in you' which actually means 'I don't care about your concerns.'

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Exactly.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Did anyone else push back?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Marcus tried but she steamrolled him. You know how she gets in that mode.
messages.notes: Coworker Marcus tried to push back

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Yeah. Once Vanessa decides something, reasoning with her is like arguing with a wall.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: The worst part is I actually think real-time notifications IS a good feature. Just not with a 7-day timeline.
messages.notes: Casual nuanced take on feature vs timeline

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Totally. It's a Q1 thing, not a 'surprise, we're doing this in a sprint' thing.
messages.notes: Temporal: should be Q1 not this sprint

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: What do you think the VP will even care about in the demo? Honestly?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: The numbers. They always care about the numbers. Usage metrics, adoption rate, that kind of stuff.
messages.notes: Context-enabling: Riley's knowledge of VP priorities

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Right. Which we HAVE. The dashboard already shows all of that.
messages.notes: Fact: dashboard already has usage metrics

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Then just make that the star of the show. The notification thing can be 'coming soon' on a roadmap slide.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Smart. Yeah. I'll frame it that way.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: You're going to be fine. The demo will be great.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Thanks. I needed to hear that honestly.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Of course. That's what work friends are for, right?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Ha, are we work friends now?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: I mean we text outside of Slack now so I think that's legally binding.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Lol fair enough.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: How's the side project going btw? The analytics thing.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Oh, actually something really cool happened.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: What?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: One of the people I interviewed — a Head of Growth at this Series B startup called Meridian — reached out and said she'd pay for early access.
messages.notes: Life event: first potential customer interest

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Wait, seriously?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yeah! Her exact words were 'if you build this, we'd be your first customer. We're spending $3K a month on Mixpanel and barely using 20% of it.'

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Dude. That's huge. That's not just validation, that's someone with their wallet out.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I know. Her name is Asha Patel. She runs a team of like 8 growth people.
messages.notes: Customer name: Asha Patel, 8-person growth team

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Have you thought about what you'd charge her?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I'm thinking something like $200 a month for early access with a commitment to feedback. Basically design partner pricing.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: That's smart. Design partner model. Lock in the feedback loop.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Exactly. What do you think about offering a free month first though? Lower the barrier.
messages.notes: Pat seeking opinion on pricing strategy

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Hmm. I'd say no. If they're already willing to pay, charging from day one establishes value. Free stuff gets deprioritized.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: That's a really good point actually. OK, no free tier.
messages.notes: Decision: no free tier for early access

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Look at us doing business strategy during a work crisis.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Multi-tasking at its finest.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: I do want to say though — I think you should really go for this. The fact that you have someone ready to pay before you've even built it is a huge signal.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Thanks. That actually means a lot.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: OK but first, survive the Vanessa demo. One crisis at a time.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Right. Priorities. Demo first, startup empire second.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: That's the spirit. Hey I'm going for a run at lunch, want to grab coffee after?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yeah that sounds good. 1:30?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Perfect. Meet at the Blue Bottle downstairs?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Works for me. See you then.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: ugh monday

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: That bad already?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: my long run yesterday was brutal. my knee started hurting around mile 14 and I had to cut it short at 16.
messages.notes: Knee pain at mile 14, cut short at 16

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Oh no. Is it serious?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: idk. it's this dull ache on the outside of my left knee. could be IT band stuff.
messages.notes: Symptom: outside left knee, possible IT band

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Have you seen a PT or anything?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: not yet. I'm doing the runner thing where I pretend it's fine and hope it goes away.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Ha, I feel like that's not the recommended approach.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: it is not. but my friend Nadia is a PT and she said I should foam roll and take a few easy days.
messages.notes: Friend Nadia is a PT, advice: foam roll + easy days

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: You should listen to Nadia.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: yeah yeah. it's just stressful because the marathon is 5 months out and I can't afford to miss training weeks.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: What do you think about cross-training? Like swimming or biking? Lower impact.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: honestly yeah that's probably smart. there's a pool at the gym I never use.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: There you go. Data says cross-training reduces injury risk anyway.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: lol 'the data says.' you're such an analyst even about running.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I can't help it. It's a curse.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: anyway how was your weekend? did you work on the side project?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: OK so I kind of went on a tear.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: oh?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I built a landing page. Like a real one. With a waitlist form and everything.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: wait seriously?? when did you do this?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Saturday night. I started at like 8pm and looked up and it was 2am.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: lol classic flow state. what's it look like?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Pretty clean actually. I used this Tailwind template and customized it. Want to see?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: obviously yes send it

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: metricflow.io — that's the name I landed on.
messages.notes: Business name decided: MetricFlow, domain: metricflow.io

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: MetricFlow. That's actually really good. Clean and descriptive.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Thanks! I bought the domain at like 1am. Only $12. I took that as a sign.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: haha $12 domain = destiny

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Exactly. I also reached back out to Asha at Meridian to tell her I'm moving forward.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: and??

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: She's in. She wants to be the first design partner. We set up a call for next week to define her requirements.
messages.notes: Asha confirmed as design partner, requirements call next week

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: Pat. You have a customer before you have a product. That's like the dream.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: I know. I keep waiting for reality to kick in.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: don't let imposter syndrome win. this is legit.

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Thanks. Seriously.

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: ok totally different topic. the Jason and Elena wedding gift. we said we'd go in together right?

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Yeah! Did you look at the registries?

messages.speaker: Riley
messages.content: I did. OK so there's a Le Creuset Dutch oven on the Williams-Sonoma one. The 5.5 quart in 'flame' orange. It's $380.
messages.notes: Gift option: Le Creuset Dutch oven, 5.5qt, flame orange, $380

messages.speaker: Pat
messages.content: Oh that's a great gift. Everyone loves Le Creuset.

messages.speaker: Riley
